texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation of CO2 from CH4 is important for processing natural gas streams that are contaminated with large quantities of CO2, which decreases the energy content of the gas and is corrosive in the presence of moisture. Because natural gas wells are at high pressures, CO2/CH4 mixtures must be separated at high pressures, which can plasticize polymeric membranes and decrease their separation performance [1,2].
texts.text: The separation of CO2 from CH4 is important for processing natural gas streams that are contaminated with large quantities of CO2, which decreases the energy content of the gas and is corrosive in the presence of moisture. Because natural gas wells are at high pressures, CO2/CH4 mixtures must be separated at high pressures, which can plasticize polymeric membranes and decrease their separation performance [1,2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolite membranes have been investigated for CO2/CH4 separations, including zeolite T [3,4], DDR [5,6], MFI [7 -10], zeolite Y [11 -13], AlPO-18 [14], and SAPO-34 [15 -24]. Zeolite T membranes with CO2/ CH4 selectivities of approximately 150 were reported, but permeances were low (1.5 -10 � 8 mol/(m 2 s Pa)), and the feed pressure was only 0.5 MPa [3]. At 3-MPa feed pressure, a DDR membrane had a CO2/CH4 separation selectivity of 80 with a CO2 permeance of  1.1 -10 � 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) [6]. MFI membranes had higher CO2 permeances (  7 -10 � 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa)) at 3-MPa feed pressure, but the highest CO2/CH4 selectivity was only about 4 [10]. A separation selectivity of 40 was reported for a potassium-exchanged Faujasite Y membranes with a permeance of  9 -10 � 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa), but these separations were only carried out at 100-kPa feed pressure with helium as a sweep gas [13]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: SAPO-34 membranes that have CO2/CH4 separation selectivities of  100 at 295 K and pressures up to 4.6 MPa also show high molecular sieving separations of mixtures containing i-butane (0.50-nm kinetic diameter) or CF4 (0.47 nm). The separation selectivities were 15,000 -20,000 for CO2/i-butane from 295 to 420 K, and 12,000 -20,000 for CO2/CF4 for feed pressures up to 0.5 MPa at 295 K. These may be the highest gas-phase separation selectivities reported for zeolite membranes, and they are likely lower limits because of concentration polarization, leakage around O-rings, and i-butane diffusion through the silicone O-rings. The low defect concentration was also con fi rmed by spatial defect distributions measured with i-butane. Thus, less than 1% of the CH4 permeation during CO2/CH4 separations is through defects larger than 0.47 nm. Isobutane, even though it is too large to adsorb in SAPO-34 pores (0.38-nm diameter), appeared to inhibit CO2 permeation by adsorbing on the external surface. These SAPO-34 membranes also separated H2/i-butane with lower limits on selectivities of 6000 at 295 K and 10,000 -14,000 at 423 K, and they were also selective for He/CH4 separation (selectivity ¼ 20) and H2/CH4 separation (selectivity ¼ 35 -45). In these separations, both He and H2 appear to speed up CH4 permeation, as expected from molecular simulations reported previously. Also, CH4 appears to slow diffusion of CO2, He, and H2.

texts.orig: SAPO-34 membranes have been shown to be highly selective for CO2/CH4 separations, even at high feed pressures, because CO2 adsorbs more strongly than CH4 in the SAPO-34 pores and CO2 also diffuses faster because it has a smaller kinetic diameter (0.33 nm) than CH4 (0.38 nm). The kinetic diameter of CH4 is essentially the same as the SAPO-34 pore size (0.38 nm). We previously reported selectivities higher than 60 for SAPO-34 membranes at feed pressures of 4.6 MPa with permeances of 10 � 6 mol/(m 2 s Pa) [25]. The separation performance of these membranes decreased as the pressure increased because CO2 adsorption loading does not increase linearly with CO2 pressure, and the CH4 fl ux through membrane defects contributes proportionally more as the pressure increases. In addition, as the pressure increases, the CO2 fl ux increases and CO2 is depleted near the membrane surface because both the decreased feed velocity and the decreased bulk diffusivity decrease mixing with the bulk phase [26]. Also, a larger fraction of the CO2 feed permeates for the same feed rate as the CO2 fl ux increases at higher pressure. All these factors increase concentration polarization, which decreases both CO2 permeance and CO2/CH4 selectivity. Te fl on inserts in the membranes tubes were used to minimize concentration polarization by decreasing the void volume and therefore increasing the linear velocity. The inserts also decreased the diffusion distance from the bulk to the membrane surface. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective of this paper is to show that SAPO-34 membranes containing few defects can be prepared, and that the defect fl ux can be estimated by separating binary gas mixtures where one component is too large to enter the zeolite pores. The overall fl ux through defects was characterized by measuring CO2/i-butane, H2/i-butane, and CO2/CF4 separation selectivities. The CO2 and H2 molecules are small enough (0.33- and 0.29-nm kinetic diameters, respectively) that they readily diffuses through the SAPO-34 pores, which have a diameter of 0.38 nm. In contrast, i-butane, with a kinetic diameter of 0.50 nm, is too large to enter the SAPO-34 pores, and it only permeates through defects that are larger than 0.50 nm. Similarly, CF4, with a kinetic diameter of 0.47 nm, only permeates through defects larger than 0.47 nm. Thus, the i-butane and CF4 fl uxes are good measures of permeation through defects. The CO2/i-butane separations could not be carried out at high pressures because the i-butane saturation pressure is 325 kPa at 295 K. In contrast, the critical temperature of CF4 is below room temperature (227 K) and gas cylinders are supplied with a pressure of greater than 12 MPa, so CO2/CF4 mixtures were separated at higher pressures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separations were used instead of single-gas permeation of these larger molecules because their fl uxes are orders of magnitude smaller than the CO2 fl ux, and thus dif fi cult to measure without a sweep gas. In contrast, even low i-butane concentrations in a CO2/i-butane permeate mixture can be detected with a GC, and the total permeate fl ux of CO2 plus i-butane is large enough to be accurately measured. The CO2/i-butane and CO2/CF4 separation selectivities were measured as a function of temperature, feed concentration, and total pressure to characterize the contribution of defects to the overall fl ux. The H2/i-butane separation selectivity was measured as a function of temperature and H2 mole fraction. Hydrogen is signi fi cantly smaller than the SAPO-34 pores, so it diffuses faster than CO2, but it adsorbs less strongly. The SAPO-34 membrane was also evaluated for separation of H2/CH4 and He/CH4 mixtures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results reported in this paper are for one SAPO-34 membrane, but as reported previously [29], SAPO-34 membranes can be prepared with good reproducibility and the best membranes had CO2/CH4 separation selectivities of  100 at room temperature and a feed pressure of 0.3 MPa for a 50:50 mixture. At 4.6 MPa, they had lower selectivities, but selectivities were still between 70 and 100 with permeances of about 5 -10 � 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa). The SAPO-34 layer in these membranes is about 5μ m thick, as previously shown by SEM for other membranes grown using the same procedure [30]. The highly-selective separation of mixtures containing i-butane or CF4 (selectivities 4 15,000 at low pressures) for the membrane described demonstrate that the SAPO-34 membranes have few defects larger than 0.47 nm, and thus they can separate mixtures by molecular sieving. These may be the highest separation selectivities for gas mixture reported for zeolite membranes.

texts.orig: 7-mm ID, 100-nm average pore size, Inopor GmbH) were cut into 6-cm lengths and the ends were glazed using Duncan ceramic glaze at 1225 K with heating and cooling rates of 1 K/min. The supports were then placed in boiling DI water for 30 min, removed from the water and placed in fresh boiling DI water (total of four times), and dried overnight at 373 K. The supports were tightly wrapped with a layer of 0.13-mm thick Te fl on tape, and then with a layer of 25μ m thick Te fl on tape. SAPO-34 seeds were then deposited on the supports by rub-coating; dry, uncalcined seeds were rubbed onto the inside surface of the alumina tubes for about 2 min with pipe cleaners covered with seed crystals. The preparation of the SAPO-34 seeds is described elsewhere [30].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SAPO-34 layers were grown by hydrothermal synthesis. Two seeded supports were placed in an autoclave, which was then fi lled with synthesis gel to about 0.5 cm above the top of the supports. The autoclave was placed in a forced convection oven at 493 K for 6 h. The autoclave was then cooled to room temperature with fl owing tap water, and the membranes were removed and rinsed in fl owing tap water for 15 min after unwrapping the Te fl on tape. Then the tubes were soaked for 24 h in stagnant DI water (500 mL, the soaking solution was replaced with fresh DI water several times during the soaking period) and dried overnight at 393 K. This 24-h rinse was found to be critical for removing unreacted synthesis gel [30].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane gel was prepared by mixing H3PO4 (85 wt% aqueous solution), Al(OH)3, (55.7 wt% Al2O3) and DI water, and stirring the mixture for 2 h. The silica source (Ludox AS-40 colloidal silica gel, 40 wt% aqueous solution) was then added and the mixture stirred for an additional 30 min. Finally, TEAOH (35 wt % aqueous solution) was added and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. These gels were aged for 12 -15 h at 323 K while stirring before using them for synthesis. The gel composition was 0.85 Al2O3:1.0 P2O5: 0.3 SiO2: 2 TEAOH: 155 H2O.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The TEAOH structure-directing template was removed at 723 K for 6 h under vacuum because we showed previously that more template was removed in vacuum than in air at the same temperature, and permeances doubled when vacuum was used [25]. A vacuum chamber with a pressure of approximately 0.1 Pa was connected to a quartz tube that contained a membrane, and the quartz tube was placed in a ceramic tubular furnace.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Spatial fl ux distributions were measured at room temperature with a movable probe connected to a mass spectrometer. The details of the system are described elsewhere [31], and only a brief description is presented here. Membranes were mounted in a stainless steel module and sealed with silicone O-rings. Isobutane was fed to the outside of the membrane, and the end caps of the module had openings so that a mass spectrometer probe could sample the permeate gas over a small region on the inside surface of the membrane. The membrane module was attached to a manual XYZ stage to align the membrane with the probe.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeate gas was collected with a 75μ m ID fused silica capillary that was located inside a stainless steel tube (0.4-mm OD). A 2-mm OD Teflon cylinder with a 0.25-mm hole in the center was glued onto the tip of the stainless steel capillary, and the silica capillary was connected to a quadrupole mass spectrometer (SRS QMS 200). The probe tip contacted the membrane surface through a hole in a centering tube. The stainless capillary pushed the probe gently against the membrane surface as the probe translated and rotated under computer control. The sample tip was purged internally through a gap between the stainless tube and the silica capillary and externally around the Te fl on tip through a hole in the centering tube where the Te fl on tip protruded.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide/methane mixtures were separated at 295 K in a fl ow system described previously [26]. The feed pressure was between 275 kPa and 4.6 MPa, and the permeate pressure was 153 kPa. Both feed and permeate pressures were controlled by back pressure regulators. The feed fl ow rates were controlled by mass fl ow controllers. No sweep gas was used. Permeate and retentate fl ow rates were monitored with bubble fl owmeters, and compositions were determined by a SRI 8610C GC with a TC detector and a Hayesep D column at 373 K. An automated sample loop obtained samples from both the feed and permeate streams.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membranes were sealed in a stainless steel module with silicone O-rings for separations measurements. The membranes were stored in an oven at 473 K between measurements. The leak integrity of the module was veri fi ed by replacing the membrane with a solid stainless steel tube. The leak rate for a 7-MPa pressure drop across the O-rings was  0.1% of the CH4 fl ux measured for a SAPO-34 membrane for a 50/50 CO2/CH4 mixture at the same pressure drop. High feed fl ow rates and cylindrical Te fl on inserts were used to minimize concentration polarization [26] by increasing the velocity across the membrane surface. Gas entered through an axial opening at the end of the Te fl on insert and was distributed through four radially aligned holes. The retentate entered the radial holes on the downstream insert and exited the membrane through a hole in the center of the insert. Permeances were calculated using log-mean feed concentrations as the driving force since feed compositions changed signi fi cantly along the membrane axis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A second fl ow system, equipped with a HP 5890 GC with a Hayesep D column and FI and TC detectors, was used for CO2/i-butane, CO2/CF4, H2/i-butane, H2/CH4 and He/CH4 separations. This fl ow system was similar to the fi rst system and was also used for CO2/CH4 separations at lower pressures to verify that results were consistent with separation measurements in the high-pressure system. The highest system pressure was 1 MPa, but the relatively-low saturation pressure of i-butane limited the feed pressure for separations involving i-butane.

This fl ow system was similar to the fi rst system and was also used for CO2/CH4 separations at lower pressures to verify that results were consistent with separation measurements in the high-pressure system. texts.orig: The SAPO-34 crystals collected from the autoclave after membrane synthesis had a composition of Si0.072Al0.56P0.37O2, as measured by ICP. Previously, we reported a crystal composition of Si0.061Al0.48P0.46O2 using a more concentrated gel and Al(i -O -Pr)3 as a precursor instead of Al (OH)3 [21]. The Si/Al ratio of 0.13 is the same in both cases, but the crystals synthesized with Al(OH)3 had an Al/P ratio of 1.5, whereas those prepared with Al(i -O -Pr)3 had a ratio of 1.05. The high Al content is similar to that found in other studies when inorganic Al precursors such as pseudoboehemite were used [35]. The reason for the Al-rich structure is not clear, and the Al/P ratio reported in the literature varies strongly depending on details of the synthesis procedures used.

texts.orig: Li et al. showed that adsorption behavior depends on SAPO-34 composition, and lowering the Si/Al ratio in the gel increased both CO2 and CH4 loadings [21]. Even though SAPO-34 was the main component when different Si/Al ratios were used, for Si/Al ratios at 0.2 and 0.1, SAPO-5 was detected by XRD. The high CO2/CH4 selectivity of the membrane used in this study suggests that SAPO5 is not a major component in the separation layer, since the 0.8nm SAPO-5 pores are not expected to be selective for CO2/CH4 separations.

texts.orig: The CO2/CH4 separation selectivity for 50:50 CO2/CH4 mixtures decreased by about 60% to a value of  40 as the temperature increased from 293 to 423 K at 275-kPa feed pressure (Fig. 4). This decrease in selectivity is similar to what was observed in previous studies with SAPO-34 membranes prepared with Al(O -i -Pr)3 on Al2O3 supports, but those membranes were of lower quality; those selectivities decreased by about 66% from 53 to 17 [19]. The CO2 permeance decreased because the CO2 loading decreased at higher temperatures. Methane adsorbs less strongly and its loading did not decrease as much, and thus its permeances increased at higher temperatures because its diffusivity increased [22].

texts.orig: mol/(m 2 s Pa) at room temperature, and i-butane concentrations in the permeate were around 30 ppm (the estimated detection limit of the FID for i-butane was less than 1 ppm, based on the signal-to-noise ratio [37]). The CO2 permeance had a slight maximum at about 360 K. At low temperatures, loading was high but diffusivities were low. As the temperature increased, the higher diffusivity results in higher permeances, but at higher temperatures where loading is low, the permeance decreased. Such maxima for zeolite membranes have been observed previously [19,21]. The i-butane permeances remained nearly constant over the temperature range, so a maximum in selectivity was seen. Such high selectivities have not been reported previously for gas separations with zeolite membranes. Since i-butane cannot enter the zeolite pores, this separation is due to molecular sieving. Furthermore, the high selectivity at elevated temperatures confi rms that the separation is not due to preferential adsorption, because adsorption decreases as the temperature increases.

texts.orig: The CO2 permeance for the CO2/i-butane mixture was almost temperature-independent between 295 and 425 K, whereas the single-gas CO2 permeance decreased approximately linearly with temperature. The CO2 mixture permeance was about 65% of the single-gas CO2 permeance at 293 K (Fig. 5), and the two permeances were equal at 423 K. The CO2 mixture permeance became closer to the CO2 single-gas permeance as the temperature increased, most likely because i-butane adsorption on the external surface of the zeolite decreased as temperature increased. That is, i-butane apparently adsorbs suf fi ciently strongly on the external surface of SAPO-34 crystals at low temperatures to inhibit CO2 permeation, but its coverage is too low at 425 K to affect CO2 permeation.

The CO2/CF4 selectivities were high up to 0.5 MPa, the highest pressure shown in Fig. 6. The CF4 permeance decreased slightly as the pressure increased, indicating that CF4 transport was not by viscous fl ow through larger defects; otherwise the permeance would increase linearly with pressure. texts.orig: The SAPO-34 membrane also separated H2/i-butane mixtures with high selectivities, as shown in Fig. 7, but the selectivities were lower than for CO2/i-butane mixtures because H2 adsorbs less strongly than CO2 in SAPO-34 pores, and the lower H2 loading results in lower H2 permeances. Thus, i-butane might be expected to compete more effectively for adsorption sites on the external surface of the SAPO-34 crystals and slow H2 permeation more than CO2. For all temperatures, the H2 permeance in the mixture was less than the single-component permeance, which it approached at higher H2 mole fractions. However, as shown in Fig. 7a, the H2 permeance at room temperature was about 75% of its single-gas permeance at 295 K, even for a 65/35 feed mixture, similar to the 20% decrease in CO2 permeance in the presence of i-butane at the same conditions (Fig. 1). As the i-butane mole fraction decreased, the H2 permeance approached the single-gas permeance. Also, this behavior was similar at 373 K (not shown) and 423 K (Fig. 7b), where the H2 mixture permeance (for a mole fraction of 0.65) was only 80% of its single-gas permeance, even though i-butane adsorption coverage on the external surfaces of the SAPO-34 crystals should decrease signi fi cantly as the temperature increased to 423 K.

texts.orig: The low i-butane and CF4 permeances indicate that the membrane has few defects larger than 0.47 nm. The spatial distribution of the i-butane fl ux (Fig. 8) indicates that the membrane does not have large defects since the i-butane mass spectrometer signal was at the detection limit across the membrane surface. The fraction of CH4 permeation through defects larger than 0.47 nm for CO2/CH4 mixtures at room temperature and low pressure is estimated to be about 1% of the CH4 permeation through the zeolite pores based on the i-butane permeances. This assumes that CH4 transports through these defects by Knudsen diffusion, although it is likely a combination of molecular, surface, and Kudsen diffusion. Knudsen diffusion was used to make an order-of-magnitude estimate because the transport parameters for molecular and surface diffusion are not known. Also, the measured i-butane permeances are upper limits as discussed above, and thus a more detailed analysis is not justi fi ed.

texts.orig: The SAPO-34 membrane separated He/CH4 mixtures with selectivities greater than 20. Both molecules are smaller than the SAPO-34 pores, but the larger CH4 diffuses more slowly, and this more than compensates for its stronger adsorption. As shown in Fig. 9, the selectivity decreased as the He mole fraction increased at 295 K and a feed pressure of 275 kPa. At low He mole fractions (high CH4 mole fractions) in Fig. 9, the CH4 mixture permeances were similar to the single-component permeances, but at high He mole fractions, the CH4 mixture permeances exceed the singlecomponent permeance. The same trends were observed for a feed pressure of 400 kPa, as shown in Fig. 10. Krishna et al. [32] used simulations to determined transport coef fi cients of mixtures through SAPO-34 membranes from permeation data. They found that the CH4 transport coef fi cient, in the presence of lighter molecules such as H2, signi fi cantly exceeds that of pure CH4. This suggested that light molecules speed up diffusion of heavier molecules in SAPO-34 pores. Concurrently, the heavier CH4 may slow He diffusion. Stronger adsorption of CH4 may also decrease He adsorption. Concentration polarization, which should decrease as the CH4 concentration decreases, is not likely to cause this behavior, since the deviation from the single-gas permeance increases as the CH4 mole fraction decreases (Fig. 9). In addition, the same deviation was found at 400 kPa (Fig. 10), where concentration polarization is more likely because of lower bulk diffusivity due to higher gas density and lower linear velocity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SAPO-34 membranes can be prepared with few defects larger than 0.47 nm; therefore they can separate gas mixtures by molecular sieving. Selectivities from 6000 to 19,000 were measured, at pressure drops from 190 to 415 kPa and temperatures from 295 to 423 K, for binary mixtures (CO2/i-butane, H2/i-butane, and CO2/CF4) that contain one component that is too large to fi t into the SAPO-34 pores. These are believed to be the highest gasphase separation selectivities reported for zeolite membranes, and they are likely lower limits because concentration polarization, leakage around O-rings, and diffusion through O-rings may all decrease selectivity. Less than 0.01% of the CO2 fl ux at 275 kPa feed pressure is through defects larger than 0.5 nm. The SAPO-34 membrane also separated He/CH4 (selectivities greater than 20) and H2/CH4 mixtures (selectivities greater than 30) over a range of feed pressures and mole fractions.
